$comment: SMGP team-season history capsules, season 1 (SMGP-024). Base-universe history: the world's own arc, NOT the player career. DISPLAY-ONLY, never a fold input.

team.madonna.summary: The tenth summer opened with the ladder carved to Madonna's measurements, and the freeze suited the house: with every drawing office locked, preparation became the whole war, and nobody prepares like the yellow-and-red. A. Senna made the drivers' argument a private tutorial, his 150th start at Great Britain read aloud to a silent grandstand, while A. Asselin closed the door with a butler's courtesy. The PALM 190 V10 never coughed; the MADONNA 456 never argued. The one shadow the garage noticed wore a backstreet address, and the crown answered as it knows: distance.
team.madonna.objective: Defend the dynasty through the freeze and make the pure drivers' argument a royal formality.
team.madonna.definingEvent: Senna's 150th Grand Prix start at Great Britain, the King's arithmetic read aloud to a grandstand too quiet for comfort.
team.madonna.carryForward: An unexamined crown grows heavy: the house enters the freeze's second winter with Bullets legal, louder, and aimed.

team.firenze.summary: Second to the crown and to no one else, the wine-dark house treated the frozen formula as vindication: when machinery stops moving, breeding is the last currency. F. Elssler banked his cold arithmetic without a wasted word, while I. Germi minted track position at the tight rounds and turned the faithful's afternoons into opera. The FIRENZE 500 left the garage like Renaissance silverwork, the FIRENZE 99 V12 singing its high, dizzy song where the straights allowed. The mood was proud and faintly pricked: by autumn, the drawing office knew a chequebook was being aimed at it.
team.firenze.objective: Hold the second throne, press the crown wherever the freeze allows, and keep the drawing office their own.
team.firenze.definingEvent: The autumn whispers that a winter chequebook was being drawn up with the house's senior engineers named on it.
team.firenze.carryForward: A cold war declared in everything but name: the aristocrats spend the winter locking doors and counting silver.

team.millions.summary: The loudest garage in the paddock spent the tenth summer learning what money cannot fix: a rulebook that froze the machinery and told the drivers to settle it. N. Jones brought the mouth, G. Alberti the breeding of a champion, and the chrome MILLIONS 189 ran up front often enough to keep the invoices defensible. The DICK MD V10 gave a headline top end and a thirsty bottom one. What the season taught the money men was insulting: the crown is not for sale. Their answer, by autumn, was to buy the people who build around it instead.
team.millions.objective: Spend the establishment dizzy and turn front-row regularity into a genuine title argument.
team.millions.definingEvent: The autumn decision to triple the budget and aim the winter chequebook at Firenze's drawing office.
team.millions.carryForward: A raid planned in secret, three signatures targeted, and the whispers reaching every motorhome before Monaco.

team.bestowal.summary: The given house ran its given season: correct, heavy, unflustered, and waiting in the queue for a crown it believes was owed at christening. M. Blume posted his metronome laps and A. Picos his warm Sunday conversions, the BESTOWAL 167 planting its sealed VAPOR DN down every long straight like weather arriving early. The slow corners stayed the car's honest compromise, the wet a correct lap instead of a brave one. The freeze offended the engineers more than the accountants: a drawing office forbidden to buy its way forward is a filing cabinet with opinions.
team.bestowal.objective: Turn correctness into silverware and remind the establishment that the gift came with expectation.
team.bestowal.definingEvent: The homologation lock itself, which froze the 167's compromises in place and made the house a student of the rulebook's next shape.
team.bestowal.carryForward: A file of long-straight data, a voice raised for a horsepower formula, and patience wearing gunmetal.

team.blanche.summary: At the top of LEVEL B the whitecoats ran the season the way they run everything: gloves on, floor mopped, every bolt logged, and not one self-inflicted wound all year. J. Herbin drove the DELTA 103 V10's honesty like a man braking by the tachometer, stringing top-fives the aristocrats pretended not to notice, while P. White chased his second miracle with a believer's patience. The BLANCHE 582 remained the grid's cleanest argument that discipline is a speed of its own. The wet days still refused them, the door stayed shut, and Tyrant's fists sharpened the feud.
team.blanche.objective: Be spotless when the establishment's door finally swings, and keep Tyrant's knuckles off the paintwork.
team.blanche.definingEvent: Herbin's run of unmarked, unbroken rounds that turned the well-mannered nuisance into a name the grandees checked for.
team.blanche.carryForward: A spotless ledger, a locked door, and a garage convinced the next tenth belongs to them.

team.tyrant.summary: LEVEL B's iron fist proved the boot camp works: M. Hamano read the crossovers like his own pulse, and G. Gould drove at the island's walls the way other men drive at corners, all or the Armco, and mostly all. The TYRANT 548 was set up hard and late around the one thing the house trusts, brakes built to hold a heartbeat longer than the other man's nerve, and the LIZZIE 24 V8 sang its narrow band until asked to fight V10s all afternoon. The door to LEVEL A never opened. The mood below decks: it will be opened.
team.tyrant.objective: Bloody every nose in LEVEL B and make the establishment hear the knocking.
team.tyrant.definingEvent: Gould's island raid in the very shadow of the wall of kings, the gunmetal car stealing the day from the polished houses.
team.tyrant.carryForward: A feud with Blanche now written into both garages' walls, and a winter spent making the brakes crueler.

team.losel.summary: The wind-tunnel house treated the freeze as an equation with one correct answer and set about solving it with setup sheets instead of drawings. E. Pacheco drove like a proof read aloud, W. Dehehe kept the blue needle on its line, and the LOSEL 125 produced the eeriest consistency in the midfield, lap after identical lap. The VAPOR DNPQ V8's virtue was that its own mechanics could open it; its ceiling was that nothing secret waited inside. The data kept saying the uncomfortable thing: they could out-calculate LEVEL B and still not beat the fire coming up from Bullets.
team.losel.objective: Solve the frozen formula on paper and bank the promotion argument the arithmetic keeps promising.
team.losel.definingEvent: The afternoon the model said a LEVEL A scalp was possible and the stopwatch agreed, once.
team.losel.carryForward: More data, sharper sheets, and a grudging line in the winter report admitting that wanting it cannot yet be graphed.

team.may.summary: The youngest crew on the grid arrived like a thaw and left believed in. G. Turner waved at the crowds on the warm-up lap, then raced the MAY 555 with a cheerfulness the midfield found maddening, because the little green car kept blooming down the straights on warm afternoons. The LORRY 32 V8 is freight-engineering with a racing number and unburstable, and May's trick was keeping it in its window and smiling. A first taste of champagne came and went; the hothouse flowers at Azalea and Iris pretended not to notice. Spring does not wait.
team.may.objective: Climb one more rung of LEVEL B and make the crowned flowers learn the newcomer's name.
team.may.definingEvent: A sun-baked afternoon the green car gatecrashed the champagne and the grandstand decided the blossom was theirs.
team.may.carryForward: Belief, a banner, and a ledger still too thin for the bills the bigger eras bring.

team.bullets.summary: The backstreet garage entered the tenth summer under protest and ended it as the establishment's favourite nightmare. The stewards stripped the BULLETS 560 bolt by bolt, declared the featherweight build legal, and G. Ceara proved the paperwork right, launching quicker than anything and dragging LEVEL C bodywork into LEVEL A arguments. The price was usual: the gearbox lived on a highwire, and smoke wrote its share of the story. The LIZZIE 24 V8 was leaned on harder than any of its three houses dared. The grandees stopped laughing by midsummer; the lock-up counted that as a podium.
team.bullets.objective: Survive the scrutineers, then raid the grandees until the crown sweats.
team.bullets.definingEvent: The stewards' bolt-by-bolt verdict before a racing lap: the loaded gun declared legal, the protest swallowed whole.
team.bullets.carryForward: Bellini's grudge now public sport, a hundred career starts banked, and a featherweight the establishment must race rather than regulate.

team.dardan.summary: The clan learned the difference between a protest and a war. Their bolt-by-bolt case against the sister-chassis Bullets was examined, argued, and dismissed by midsummer, the stewards unable to name a single sin in the featherweight build, and E. Bellini took the verdict as Dardan takes everything: as an opening shot. The DARDAN 700, oxblood over hammered bronze, was sharpened for the walled streets and drew blood there, K. Alfven banking what Bellini's temper spent. The VAPOR DNPQ V8 gave honest customer torque, no mysteries. The grudge outlived the paperwork; by early summer the pit wall had stopped pretending otherwise.
team.dardan.objective: Prove the sister chassis was stolen thunder, and storm the LEVEL C midfield regardless.
team.dardan.definingEvent: The failed protest: the loaded gun declared legal while the spear was still in the clan's hand.
team.dardan.carryForward: A public feud, a paddock choosing sides, and a winter of paperwork being sharpened into hearings.

team.linden.summary: The oldest workshop on the grid kept its own time: M. Moreau read circuits like weather, brought the deep-green LINDEN LN198 home through everything the season threw, and passed one hundred and twenty starts, all for the same house, a bond the almanac has never matched. The LORRY 32 V8 was seasoned like firewood until it forgot how to break, and the car remained the last machine anyone would see smoking against the Armco. Points came the farmer's way, banked while flashier garages planted and burned. The Bullets sapling stormed past all year; the old tree did not rustle.
team.linden.objective: Endure, harvest, and deepen the longest single-team tenure the championship has ever kept.
team.linden.definingEvent: Start number one hundred and twenty, every one in Linden green, noted by the paddock with something close to reverence.
team.linden.carryForward: The old iron's keeper anointed by default: when the frozen era ends, everyone already knows who should lead its farewell.

team.minarae.summary: The championship's front door held as advertised: B. Miller turned the concrete canyons into Minarae country, brake-late and elbows-out until the leaders found no clean air, and J. Nono ate hairpins for breakfast often enough to keep the promise louder than the results. The MINARAE 594 is courage with a number on the nose, and the SEGA SG1000 V8 remains the grid's best first teacher, mild, honest, impossible to kill. Horsepower stayed the ceiling; the twisty stuff stayed the argument. The on-ramp's purpose showed in the scouts' notebooks: both cars left every circuit with somebody's name underlined.
team.minarae.objective: Launch reputations upward and make LEVEL B's doorkeepers bid against each other.
team.minarae.definingEvent: Miller's canyon afternoon, the little car defending against the front of the grid until the tannoy ran out of ways to say it.
team.minarae.carryForward: Two names underlined in every scout's notebook, and the banner's three painted words holding for another year.

team.rigel.summary: At the floor's upper edge the museum rolled out its midnight-blue ghost and asked the light back. R. Cotman drove like a man at peace with the back rows but not surrendered to them, T. Chardin carried himself like fallen nobility because that is the garage, and the RIGEL 3000, beautiful and brittle, produced its customary festivals: a points finish celebrated like a podium, a clean qualifying run framed on the wall. The LORRY 32 V8 kept its superstitions about finishing and mostly honoured them. The trophies gathered dust at the correct angle. The star, everyone insisted, still burns.
team.rigel.objective: Hold the cruel rung above the abyss and steal one more festival for the museum wall.
team.rigel.definingEvent: A points finish that sent the lifers searching the spares shelf for the good bottle.
team.rigel.carryForward: The same war, one season older: keep the ghost above Zeroforce and the light in view.

team.comet.summary: The romantics of the floor spent the season chasing their own tail-light. E. Tornio dragged the COMET 323 to one qualifying slot that made the timing screens gasp, and for two flying laps the matte-black car looked like The Season come back around. Then reliability told the truth: the LIZZIE 24 V8, eager and light, was asked to live past half-distance and declined with the usual plume. The spares shelf stayed bare, the highlight reel stayed framed, and the basement quarrels with Cool and Moon ran all year. Hope, in this garage, is a maintenance schedule, strictly kept.
team.comet.objective: Steal a shock from the back rows and keep the memory from cooling another season.
team.comet.definingEvent: Tornio's qualifying flyer, two laps where the old comet-tail burned bright enough to silence the pit lane.
team.comet.carryForward: The same bet, placed again: that a comet comes back around before the floor gives way.

team.orchis.summary: The glasshouse flower survived, which was the whole season. C. Tegner coaxed the pale ORCHIS 056 through the cool afternoons, when it cornered like petals opening, and nursed it home through the hot ones, when the MISFIRE 50 V8 drank its cooling and sulked. The floor's ledge crumbled beneath them all year, Zeroforce waiting below with nothing beneath it, and the crew kept pointing, quickly, almost pleading, at the days the bloom was real. A points finish in kind weather became the garage's whole argument for existence. The frost comes for the tender ones first; the greenhouse knows it.
team.orchis.objective: Survive every Sunday and bloom once more before the frost arrives.
team.orchis.definingEvent: A cool, smooth afternoon when the flower opened properly and the big board had to make room for it.
team.orchis.carryForward: A driver learning to read skies instead of stopwatches, and a garage praying for weather.

team.zeroforce.summary: The end of the road kept its pit box, which at this address is the entire trophy case. P. Klinger, patient because patience costs nothing down here, brought the blacked-out ZEROFORCE 231 home when it agreed to go home, and the LORRY 32 V8, the one part of the car with no irony in it, simply went. Qualifying was the weekly trial, finishing the weekly miracle, not being last at the flag the weekly festival. The crew's gallows humour stayed darker than the livery. Every other garage uses this team as a mirror it avoids. The mirror kept its nerve.
team.zeroforce.objective: Qualify, finish, refuse the bottom, and keep the lights on one more month.
team.zeroforce.definingEvent: The afternoon the zero on the flank was not the verdict: a flag taken with someone else behind it.
team.zeroforce.carryForward: Nothing sold, nothing surrendered; the floor holds, and the grey dignity goes again.

team.joke.summary: The carnival ran its favourite con: sandbag the Saturday, clown for the pit boards, dawdle a second off the pace until the big board scratched the #16 off the reckoning, then arrive at Sunday's hairpin with the throttle buried and a wave for the grandstand. L. Dufay turned the punchline into points twice and swore blind he had greased the hairpin himself. The JOKE 777's POND V8, a marine engine that wandered into the circus, was practically impossible to drown and barely possible to hurry, which suited the theatre. LEVEL B stopped laughing weeks before the establishment did.
team.joke.objective: Spoil the grandees' afternoons and keep the whole LEVEL B pack guessing.
team.joke.definingEvent: The Sunday charge from a sandbagged grid slot into the points, executed with a wave at the people it embarrassed.
team.joke.carryForward: The paddock's attention, which was the plan, and the stewards' attention, which will cost.

team.lares.summary: The hearth kept its lamp lit all summer. P. Arai drove the stout LARES 92 with a gentleman's patience, and the RAM V12 beneath him, geological and without stress, made the car a law of nature: it came back running, lap after lap, while quicker machines wrote themselves off around it. The speed traps told the honest story and the finishing column the better one. The Bullets insurgents snarled past; the neighbourly feud never turned ugly, the household was here first and intends to be here after. Points scavenged when the fancy teams broke: the guardian's record, growing.
team.lares.objective: Keep the warm middle rung, finish everything, and shelter the climbers who pass through.
team.lares.definingEvent: A finishing streak long enough that rivals grumbled the red car would outlast the catering.
team.lares.carryForward: The lamp still lit, the record still growing, and a patient man quietly becoming the paddock's favourite neighbour.

team.feet.summary: The working men's team out-worked the year, the only plan it has ever had. J. Rampal clawed every tenth out of the FEET 13 as if the seat were the only thing keeping his lights on, because it nearly is, and Old Boots dragged itself home on its rims while prettier machinery fed the treeline. The YOUGEN V10 is industrial honesty, and the garage hand-fettled what the chequebook could not buy. On one chaos afternoon the battered blue car came home ahead of a machine costing ten times as much, and the pit lane tipped its cap.
team.feet.objective: Bank honest points, out-last the silver spoons, and graft one rung upward.
team.feet.definingEvent: The chaos afternoon Old Boots beat a grandee's car home and the paddock's mechanics applauded.
team.feet.carryForward: Calloused hands, an honest ledger, and a dream of LEVEL B spoken low over garage tea.

team.serga.summary: The one-man mountain banditry continued: E. Sambena built it, funded it, drove it, and proved the gate onto pit lane opens for whoever welds hard enough. The SERGA 1000 rattled where the factory cars purred and filled their mirrors anyway, the SC3000 F12 mountain engine eager once warmed and opinionated about its oil on long corners. Every point was banked, never bought, and the grandstand roared each time the grey car beat machinery worth ten of it home. The spares shelf was the quiet enemy; Sambena rebuilt at midnight what the aristocrats replaced. Nobody backs Serga, which remains the point.
team.serga.objective: Make the whole grid admit a garage can race, and steal a salaried man's comfort by Sunday.
team.serga.definingEvent: The grey #25 finishing ahead of a factory machine, painted number slightly crooked, grandstand on its feet.
team.serga.carryForward: A ledger already thinner than the spares shelf, and a privateer's pride that will not admit it.

team.cool.summary: The calmest garage in the circus set out the deckchairs and had a wonderful season by its own books. A. Delvaux called every hairpin a love letter, drove the glacier-blue COOL 05 with a dreamer's throttle, and treated a single point as a title settled at sunset. The CORSE V8 ran when loved and sulked in its electrics at the worst moments, which the crew accepted like weather. It coughed to a halt often enough to keep the mechanics humble, finished often enough to keep the postcards coming. The big board said what it liked; Cool read it slowly.
team.cool.objective: Turn up, go racing, enjoy the ride, and never once let the panic in.
team.cool.definingEvent: A solitary point, celebrated at the cheap end of the paddock like a crown.
team.cool.carryForward: The deckchairs, the cooler, and the quiet truth that staying is winning.

team.moon.summary: The far side of the grid kept its cold vigil and finished more often than the flashier wreckers. K. Yepes waved at the machines that lapped him and insisted the back row has the finest view on the grid, and the MOON 292, silver and crater-dusted, circled patiently underneath. The RAM V12 is a bridge, overbuilt and unburstable, and Moon's season was built on it: keep circling long after the eager cars have broken. The points column stayed empty as deep space, and the pit-wall moonshots came to nothing, fondly. Only Zeroforce sat below. The telescope stayed pointed up.
team.moon.objective: Keep the lights on, keep circling, and plot the slow ascent one phase at a time.
team.moon.definingEvent: A finish under the Monaco lamps, the loneliest, prettiest car on the grid still running at the end.
team.moon.carryForward: The same orbit, faithfully kept, and the unkillable wish to rise.

team.iris.summary: The jewel-cut house ran its precise masterpiece of a season and measured it, as always, against the crown. B. Salgado, a champion who wants his crown back before the fire cools, told the white-glove garage that in winter, then drove like the sentence was a contract: the IRIS 717 knifed through the fast sweepers where lesser machines washed wide, and the PRISM 90 V10 sang its clean, expensive note where only exact hands may visit. Race-day economy against the PALM 190 remained the honest gap. The treasury of podiums grew; the ledger against Madonna did not move.
team.iris.objective: Be the closest jewel to the throne and make Salgado's vow a programme, not a promise.
team.iris.definingEvent: Salgado's winter declaration to his own white-glove garage: the crown, back, before the fire cools.
team.iris.carryForward: A champion's hunger on file, the sister war quietly formal, and the gap to the crown measured to the tenth.

team.azalea.summary: The frost flower was underestimated on schedule. M. Larssen, the ice at its heart, drove the early rounds with a patience the paddock mistook for decoration, and then the AZALEA 808 opened on the grand afternoons, the BLOOM 88 V8 building like a held breath and arriving at full song when the stage was worth it. The sister war with Iris ran beneath everything, the loud sister headlining, the glacial one collecting the results that end arguments. The crown's garage checked its mirrors on the big nights and found the pink car there. The cabinet holds the biggest days.
team.azalea.objective: Wait for the stages worth flowering on, and end the season owning them.
team.azalea.definingEvent: The grand afternoon the bloom opened over the establishment and the loud sister went quiet.
team.azalea.carryForward: A warning issued in frost: the family now has two jewels, and the colder one cuts deeper.
